input.background: A nuclear plant is located in an area susceptible to both high winds and earthquakes. From the historical data, the mean frequency of large earthquakes capable of damaging important plant structures is one every 50 years. The corresponding frequency of damaging high winds is once in 25 years. During a strong earthquake, the probability of structure damage is 0.1. During high winds, the damage probability is 0.05. Assume that earthquakes and high winds can be described by independent Poisson random variables and that the damages caused by these events are independent.
input.question: What is the probability of having strong winds but not large earthquakes during a 10-year period?

output.answer: Let the random variable $X$ and $Y$ represent the number of earthquakes and the number of occurrences of high winds, respectively. We assume that the two random variables are statistically independent. The mean of $X$ and $Y$ are, respectively, given by $$\lambda_X=\frac{1}{50 \ \text { years }}(10 \ \text { years })=0.2$$ and $$\lambda_Y=\frac{1}{25 \ \text { year }}(10 \ \text { years })=0.4$$. The conditional damage probabilities are given as follows: $$P(\ \text { damage/earthquake })=0.1$$ and $$P(\ \text { damage } / \ \text { wind })=0.05$$. Let the event $A=\{$ strong winds and no earthquakes $\}$. Assuming that winds and earthquakes are independent, the probability of having strong winds but not earthquakes during the 10-year period can be written as $$\begin{aligned} P(A) & =P(w \ \text { inds }) P(\ \text { no earthquakes }) \\ & =[1-P(\ \text { no winds })] P(\ \text { no earthquakes }) \end{aligned}$$. Therefore, we obtain $$P(A)=\left(1-e^{-0.4}\right)\left(e^{-0.2}\right)=0.27$$.

meta_info.chapter: Chapter 2: Distribution Functions and Its Applications
